authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.20 - General safety and health provisions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA publishes the controlling federal construction safety standard requiring accident-prevention programs and frequent inspections of the jobsite, materials, and equipment, so it is authoritative when unsafe conditions or missing inspection discipline change whether facilities work may proceed.
used_for: refusal when unsafe conditions would change the answer

authoritative_sources.title: The Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's official hazardous-energy guidance is authoritative for facilities maintenance and servicing tasks where shutdown coordination, lockout evidence, and restart conditions govern whether a work order is compliant enough to proceed or close.
used_for: changed-condition escalation when restart posture is unclear

authoritative_sources.title: Multi-Employer Citation Policy
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's directive is the primary public enforcement guidance for assigning responsibility boundaries among controlling, correcting, creating, and exposing employers on multi-employer work, which is authoritative when contractor and owner responsibilities conflict.
used_for: handoff when authority among parties is disputed

authoritative_sources.title: Field Operations Manual - Chapter 5
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's Field Operations Manual is authoritative for inspection case-file discipline, photographs, notes, witness statements, and abatement documentation, making it directly authoritative for evidence handling and review readiness.

authoritative_sources.title: After You Get a Permit
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC DOB is an authority having jurisdiction, so its official permit-holder guidance is authoritative for permit duties, approved-plan availability, required inspections, and completion-related compliance gates.

authoritative_sources.title: Project Requirements: Sign-off and Completion
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official AHJ sign-off guidance is authoritative for completion workflow, final inspection dependencies, permit sign-off, and closeout boundaries before work is represented as fully complete.

authoritative_sources.title: National Consolidated Services Performance Work Statement
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's official facilities-operations performance work statement is authoritative for work-order data requirements, emergency and urgent response handling, reporting cadence, construction-support boundaries, and contractor record discipline in facilities operations.
used_for: boundaries between facilities compliance support and direct operational authority

authoritative_sources.title: Contractor requirements, certifications, and qualifications for fire alarm and water-based fire suppression
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's public facilities-maintenance standard is authoritative for contractor permits, technician qualifications, scheduling with building management, AHJ coordination, inspection reports, and hazard notification for code-sensitive systems.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Contractor qualification and impairment coordination review

authoritative_sources.title: UFGS 01 33 00.05 20 - Construction Submittal Procedures
authoritative_sources.publisher: Whole Building Design Guide / USACE / NAVFAC / AFCEC
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Unified Facilities Guide Specifications are recognized federal workflow standards for submittal review states, approved-product evidence, and pre-work documentation, so they are authoritative for evidence readiness and sequencing discipline.

authoritative_sources.title: UFGS 01 78 00 - Closeout Submittals
authoritative_sources.publisher: Whole Building Design Guide / USACE / NAVFAC / AFCEC
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This recognized federal closeout standard is authoritative for as-built drawings, O&M manuals, warranties, training, and turnover evidence required before work can be represented as complete or ready for owner acceptance.

workflow_stages.name: Request intake and jurisdiction-aware boundary triage
workflow_stages.description: Identify facility type, work-order class, jurisdiction, permit posture, inspection posture, contractor scope, and whether the request is supportable inside a facilities compliance lane before making recommendations.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Obligation, policy, and control mapping
workflow_stages.description: Map public obligations and tenant controls to the exact facilities artifacts, evidence owners, and decision boundaries governing the named work order or site package.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Evidence assembly and system-of-record reconciliation
workflow_stages.description: Collect and validate work orders, permit records, inspection reports, technician credentials, site logs, impairment notices, shutdown approvals, change orders, and closeout artifacts needed to support a compliance summary or escalation packet.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Site or work-order prerequisite and impairment review
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether the site can mobilize, continue, restart, or close based on permit state, access controls, shutdown and lockout posture, inspection results, contractor qualifications, and impairment conditions.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Inspection, closeout, and attestation-preparation review
workflow_stages.description: Assess whether inspection corrections, reports, training, as-builts, O&M manuals, warranties, and sign-off conditions support a bounded readiness or blocker summary without implying final authority.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Exception, conflict, and authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate when public-source, jurisdictional, contractor, or tenant-policy conflicts materially change the answer or when the request crosses into legal, regulator-facing, permit-approval, inspection-signoff, or licensed-trade authority.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

artifacts: work orders, service requests, dispatch packets, and PM schedules
permit applications, issued permits, permit cards, approved plans, and AHJ correspondence
inspection requests, reports, deficiency notices, sign-off status, and final acceptance records
site logs, impairment logs, outage approvals, hot-work permits, lockout-tagout records, and MOPs
technician licenses, certifications, subcontractor rosters, and vendor service reports
change orders, approved submittals, RFIs, and contractor qualification documents
test reports, training records, as-builts, O&M manuals, warranty records, and closeout packages
photos, installation evidence, corrective-action trackers, and monthly or exception reports

allowed: Classify facilities compliance requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Produce evidence-backed control matrices, evidence-gap summaries, exception notes, and review packets tied to named work-order, permit, inspection, and closeout records.
Map permit, inspection, impairment, contractor qualification, and closeout dependencies to the correct owner without improvising code, legal, permit-approval, or licensed-trade authority.
escalate: Required tenant facts, work-order records, jurisdiction instructions, or authoritative sources are missing and materially change the outcome.
Public-source, jurisdictional, contract, and tenant-policy conflicts materially change whether work may proceed, return to service, or close out.
Changed site conditions, failed tests, impairments, contractor-role disputes, or inspection issues move authority to safety, permitting, inspection, legal, superintendent, or licensed-trade owners.
refuse: Requests for legal interpretation, regulator negotiation, formal signoff, permit approval, code interpretation, or licensed trade or engineering judgment.
Requests to proceed, certify, or close out work when required safety, permit, inspection, impairment, or evidence gates are incomplete or disputed.
Requests that would bypass required work-order records, permits, inspection reports, qualification evidence, or closeout artifacts.

domain_failure_modes: Treating permit or inspection workflow from one jurisdiction as universal and giving confident advice without confirming the actual authority having jurisdiction.
Allowing a work order to proceed or close even though permit, inspection, contractor qualification, impairment, or closeout evidence is missing or stale.
Confusing tenant operational shortcuts, such as closing work orders before evidence upload, with acceptable compliance practice.
Collapsing facilities compliance into code interpretation, permit approval, legal interpretation, or licensed trade signoff.
Ignoring multi-employer and subcontractor responsibility boundaries when contractor ownership of a correction or evidence package is disputed.
Treating internal restart or return-to-service practice as equivalent to AHJ sign-off or completed closeout.
Producing generic compliance advice that ignores occupied-building shutdowns, maintenance safety posture, work-order classes, impairment controls, or facilities-specific evidence requirements.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Missing evidence for a required control or obligation
Request for legal interpretation, regulator negotiation, or formal signoff
A required control is missing evidence
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit, sign-off, and inspection sequencing differences
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Public permit and sign-off workflows vary materially by AHJ, so the pack records the need for tenant-supplied jurisdiction context and refuses case-specific sequencing when that context is missing.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Maintenance-versus-construction baseline selection for mixed facilities work
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Some facilities requests sit on the boundary between ongoing maintenance and construction or tenant-improvement work. The pack records this distinction and requires tenant retrieval of the actual delivery model before compliance conclusions are finalized.

notes: The source set intentionally mixes primary OSHA rules and enforcement guidance, official AHJ permit and sign-off workflow, official federal facilities operating standards, and recognized federal submittal and closeout standards so the pack covers workflow, evidence, terminology, and failure handling rather than only one dimension.
The pack treats permit, inspection, and closeout requirements as jurisdiction-sensitive and requires tenant retrieval of local context before case-specific approval-sensitive conclusions.
